query: How does z-index really work in CSS?

competitors.label: z-index
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical MDN property reference for z-index, including stacking behavior, syntax, and the relationship between descendants and local stacking contexts.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on z-index syntax, applicability, and the rule that descendants stay within their parent's stacking context.
theirGaps: The property reference is less interview-shaped and less explicit about the debugging workflow teams actually use when huge z-index values still lose.

realWorldUseCases:
Debug lens

Most z-index bugs are not about picking a larger number. They happen because the wrong stacking context owns the layer order. Once opacity, transform, filters, or positioned ancestors create a new context, your huge z-index value may still lose. That is why z-index debugging is really stacking-context debugging.
followUpConfusion:
Debugging Tip
Use browser developer tools to inspect stacking contexts. Understanding which context your element belongs to is key to resolving unexpected layering issues.

realWorldUseCases: MDN defines z-index correctly, but our page is more explicit about the real production failure mode: the wrong ancestor owns the stacking context, so bigger numbers stop helping.
actionableExamples: Its canonical examples and formal property behavior keep the example criterion competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is stronger on the usual follow-up confusion: z-index bugs are really stacking-context bugs, not just insufficiently large integers.

actionableExamples: Keep the dropdown-versus-overlay debugging frame visible so the page stays operational instead of purely definitional.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical z-index reference, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it reframes z-index as a stacking-context debugging problem instead of a number-picking problem.

competitors.label: CSS Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's CSS interview bank is directly relevant because layering, positioning, and stacking behavior are recurring CSS interview topics.
theirStrengths: It is a strong CSS interview-prep hub with broad coverage of positioning and layout fundamentals.
theirGaps: The category page is not dedicated to stacking contexts, so it is less focused on the exact bug pattern that makes z-index difficult in practice.

realWorldUseCases:
Practical scenario
Ensure a dropdown appears above a sticky header and modal overlay.

Common pitfalls
  • Forgetting z-index only works on positioned elements.
  • Creating new stacking contexts with transforms or opacity.
  • Using huge z-index values without a scale.
Trade-off or test tip
Central z-index scales reduce chaos. Test overlays in combination (dropdown + modal).
followUpConfusion:
Still so complicated?

Think of z-index like layers in Photoshop — each element lives on a sheet. The higher the z-index, the closer to your eyes it appears. But if it's in a different folder (stacking context), it can’t overlap layers in another folder.

competitors.label: Z-index and stacking contexts
competitors.selectionReason: This is the strongest tutorial comparator for the exact z-index mental model because it centers stacking contexts, positioned elements, and layered UI debugging.
theirStrengths: It is strong on the connection between z-index and stacking contexts and includes concrete overlapping examples.
theirGaps: It is less compact as a single interview answer and less explicit about keeping a team-wide z-index scale for real UI systems.

realWorldUseCases:
Debug lens

Most z-index bugs are not about picking a larger number. They happen because the wrong stacking context owns the layer order. Once opacity, transform, filters, or positioned ancestors create a new context, your huge z-index value may still lose. That is why z-index debugging is really stacking-context debugging.
followUpConfusion:
Common Pitfalls
  • Setting z-index on an element without a positioning context (e.g., position: static) has no effect.
  • Nested stacking contexts can cause confusion — an element may appear behind another even if it has a higher z-index, depending on its parent’s context.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev is strong on stacking contexts, but our page is more compact about the production rule developers need first: inspect the owning context before touching the number scale.
actionableExamples: Its layered examples are strong, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is clearer on the next debugging question teams usually ask: why a child with 9999 still loses to a sibling in a higher parent context.
